coredata.dc:title: Theoretical studies on the possible sensitizers of DSSC: Nanocomposites of graphene quantum dot hybrid phthalocyanine/tetrabenzoporphyrin/tetrabenzotriazaporphyrins/cis-tetrabenzodiazaporphyrins/tetrabenzomonoazaporphyrins and their Cu-metallated macrocycles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Feng
Yang, Chuan-Lu
Wang, Mei-Shan
Ma, Xiao-Guang
Liu, Wen-Wang
coredata.dc:description: The feasibility of nanocomposites of cir-coronene graphene quantum dot (GQD) with phthalocyanine, tetrabenzoporphyrin, tetrabenzotriazaporphyrins, cis-tetrabenzodiazaporphyrins, tetrabenzomonoazaporphyrins and their Cu-metallated macrocycles as a sensitizer of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) are investigated. Based on the first principles density functional theory (DFT), the geometrical structures of the separate GQD and 10 macrocycles, and their hybridized nanocomposites are fully optimized. The energy stabilities of the obtained structures are confirmed by harmonic frequency analysis. The optical absorptions of the optimized structures are calculated with time-dependent DFT. The feasibility of the nanocomposites as the sensitizer of DSSC is examined by the charge spatial separation, the electron transfer, the molecular orbital energy levels of the nanocomposites and the electrolyte, and the conduction band minimum of TiO2 electrode. The results demonstrate that all the nanocomposites have enhanced absorptions in the visible light range, and their molecular orbital energies satisfy the requirement of sensitizers. However, only two of the ten considered nanocomposites demonstrate significantly charge spatial separation. The GQD-Cu-TBP is identified as the most favorable candidate sensitizer of DSSC by the most enhanced in optical absorption, obvious charge spatial separation, suitable LUMO energy levels and driving force for electron transfer, and low recombination rate of electron and hole.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensitizer
Charge spatial separation
Nanocomposites
Optical absorption
Electron transfer

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Geometrical Structures and Vibrational Spectra of QDs and Nanocomposites
Feasibility of Nanocomposites as Sensitizers of DSSC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The optimized structures for (a) TBP, TBMAP, TBDAP, TBTAP, Pc, Cu-TBP, Cu-TBMAP, Cu-TBDAP, Cu-TBTAP and Cu-Pc. (b) GQD-metal-free macrocycles and GQD-Cu-metallated macrocycles nanocomposites.
The vibrational spectrum for GQD-metal-free macrocycles and GQD-Cu-metallated macrocycles nanocomposites.

Energy levels and contour plots of the HOMO and LUMO for GQD-metal-free macrocycles and GQD-copper-metallated macrocycles nanocomposites, together with the energy levels of the conduction band minimum (CBM) of TiO2 and I−/I3 − represents the electrolyte.
HOMO and LUMO of GQD-metal-free macrocycles and GQD-Cu-metallated macrocycles nanocomposites.
(a). The photoabsorption spectra of GQD, metal-free macrocycles and GQD-metal-free macrocycles nanocomposites, calculated with the complex polarizability algorithm.
(b). The photoabsorption spectra of GQD, Cu-metallated macrocycles and GQD-Cu-metallated macrocycles nanocomposites, calculated with the complex polarizability algorithm.

head.ce:title: Theoretical studies on the possible sensitizers of DSSC: Nanocomposites of graphene quantum dot hybrid phthalocyanine/tetrabenzoporphyrin/tetrabenzotriazaporphyrins/cis-tetrabenzodiazaporphyrins/tetrabenzomonoazaporphyrins and their Cu-metallated macrocycles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Optoelectronics Engineering, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Optoelectronics Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The feasibility of nanocomposites of cir-coronene graphene quantum dot (GQD) with phthalocyanine, tetrabenzoporphyrin, tetrabenzotriazaporphyrins, cis-tetrabenzodiazaporphyrins, tetrabenzomonoazaporphyrins and their Cu-metallated macrocycles as a sensitizer of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) are investigated. Based on the first principles density functional theory (DFT), the geometrical structures of the separate GQD and 10 macrocycles, and their hybridized nanocomposites are fully optimized. The energy stabilities of the obtained structures are confirmed by harmonic frequency analysis. The optical absorptions of the optimized structures are calculated with time-dependent DFT. The feasibility of the nanocomposites as the sensitizer of DSSC is examined by the charge spatial separation, the electron transfer, the molecular orbital energy levels of the nanocomposites and the electrolyte, and the conduction band minimum of TiO2 electrode. The results demonstrate that all the nanocomposites have enhanced absorptions in the visible light range, and their molecular orbital energies satisfy the requirement of sensitizers. However, only two of the ten considered nanocomposites demonstrate significantly charge spatial separation. The GQD-Cu-TBP is identified as the most favorable candidate sensitizer of DSSC by the most enhanced in optical absorption, obvious charge spatial separation, suitable LUMO energy levels and driving force for electron transfer, and low recombination rate of electron and hole.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, searching for renewable and environmentally friendly energy sources has become a current topic of interest due to the continuous depletion of fossil fuels and serious environmental issues. With the potential of becoming a clean and renewable energy source, dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) have drawn much attention because of their relatively high photoelectronic absorption efficiencies and lower production cost. DSSC has been regarded as one of the most promising alternatives for traditional silicon-based solar cells because of their facile fabrication process, and the possibility of large-scale manufacture [1]. However, an efficient sensitizer is needed to harvest the solar energy and to transfer electrons in DSSC effectively. In recent years, many groups are devoted to the development of the novel sensitizer to improve the efficiency of DSSC. Among the various sensitizers of organic dyes, phthalocyanine (Pc) is a very robust one with its extraordinary high extinction coefficients in the 600 to 700 nm light region [2–4]. Moreover, Pc is thermally and chemically stable and has appropriate redox properties for sensitization of TiO2 films. Hence, it is regarded as a perfect light-harvesting material for light-to-energy conversion devices, and remarkable progress has been made in the use of Pc as a sensitizer of DSSC [5–10].

Additionally, the semiconductor tetrabenzoporphyrin (TBP) also is a potential sensitizer of DSSC because of the intense and tunable absorption in the red or near-infrared region, transparency over the high energy portion of the visible range [3]. TBP has a strong peak linear attenuation coefficient and can absorb a significant fraction of light [11]. In 2009, Matsuo et al. [12] reported TBP was used to make solution-processed organic solar cells with a relatively high efficiency of 5.2%. Noguchi and co-workers [13] reported TBP organic thin film showing that the carrier mobility is three times higher for the film prepared at a lower temperature than that prepared at higher temperatures. In 2015, Dao et al. [14] investigated the enhancement efficiency of organic solar cells by utilizing various phthalocyanine–TBP hybrid macrocycles.

Although the Pc and TBP can be used as sensitizers, they lack strong absorption in the most part of the visible light region, which limits them as the highly efficient sensitizers of DSSC because the satisfactory efficient sensitizer should be strong absorption in the range from UV to infrared wavelength, especially the visible light range. Therefore, to make Pc and TBP become more effective sensitizer, one should modify Pc and TBP with some functional light absorbing materials or construct nanocomposites with other molecules to enhance the optical absorption in the visible light range.

The nanocomposites of dyes and quantum dots (QD) proves to be efficient to harvest solar energy, because the nanocomposites are superior light absorption properties as compared to the individual components from which it is made [15,16]. Among the various nanocomposites of QDs, the ones made of the graphene quantum dot (GQD) are received great attention, which makes them as a potential candidate for the fabrication of nanocomposites with favorable properties. In 2010, Li et al. [17] through their pioneering work have shown that periodic graphene can also be realized in the form of QDs. GQD has also many particular properties, which makes it become a promising candidate material for next-generation ultra-thin optoelectronic devices and building blocks of the future electronics devices [18–20]. Unlike the bulk graphene, GQD has a band energy gap because of quantum confinement [21,22] and exhibit strong photoluminescence [23–25]. GQD applications in renewable energy resources like photovoltaics [26], in electronics and optoelectronics such as photodetector [27] and phototransistor [28] have already been demonstrated by many types of research.

In fact, GQD can possibly be used as a sensitizer or co-sensitizer of DSSC. Very recently, Tsai et al. [29] report that a high efficiency of 16.55% in n-type Si heterojunction solar cells can be achieved by employing GQD as a sensitizer. In 2015, Mihalache et al. [30] reported an efficiency enhancement of DSSC using N3 dye co-sensitized with GQDs. They achieved an overall cells efficiency of 2.15% compared to that of the reference efficiency 1.92%. By combined GQDs and N719 dye, Fang et al. [31] obtained a better DSSC efficiency of 6.1% with a net PCE improvement of ~19% compared with the reference DSSC efficiency of 5.1%.

Considering GQD, Pc and TBP can be used as a sensitizer or co-sensitizer for DSSC, one can construct nanocomposites of Pc and TBP with QD to strengthen the solar energy absorption. At the same time, tetrabenzotriazaporphyrins (TBTAP), cis-tetrabenzodiazaporphyrins (TBDAP), tetrabenzomonoazaporphyrins (TBMAP) can be regarded as hybrid structures of Pc and TBP. Moreover, the optical properties of the Cu-metallated macrocycles mentioned above, representing by Cu-TBP, Cu-TBMAP, Cu-TBDAP, Cu-TBTAP, and Cu-Pc have also been investigated [32,33]. However, the feasibility of nanocomposites of GQD and the above macrocycles as sensitizers of DSSC has not been explored. To evaluate the possibility of these nanocomposites as a sensitizer of DSSC, we optimize their geometrical structures with the hybrid density functional theory (DFT) methods and confirm the energy stability with frequency analysis. The optical absorption properties are calculated with the time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) method [34]. The results demonstrated that the optical absorption of the nanocomposites is significantly enhanced in comparison to those of the GQD or the macrocycles. After analyzing the energy level and electronic density of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), the charge spatial separation and the optical absorption in the visible light range, we identify that the nanocomposites of GQD with Pc or Cu-TBP are the most potential candidate sensitizers of DSSC. These findings can provide a helpful guide for developing efficient DSSC with nanocomposites of GQD and macrocycles.

The cir-coronene C54H18 structure is used to stand for the present GQD. The present specific size is chosen according to the detail discussion about the structural parameters and optical properties by Li et al. [35]. The larger GQD including more C atoms (for examples 96 atoms which are often used to investigate one GQD in the literature) will make the demand on computing resources for the large nanocomposites. Five macrocycles including Pc, TBP, TBTAP, TBDAP, TBMAP, and GQD are considered as shown in Fig. 1(a). Pc and TBP are organic compounds with the formula (C8H4N2)4H2 and C36H22N4, respectively. Moreover, the Cu-metallated macrocycles mentioned above, representing by Cu-TBP, Cu-TBMAP, Cu-TBDAP, Cu-TBTAP, and Cu-Pc are also shown in Fig. 1(a). The nanocomposites consisting of GQD and 10 macrocycles are constructed by grafting one phenyl ring of the macrocycles on the GQD through an amide linkage. The geometrical configurations of nanocomposites are optimized using hybrid DFT method implemented in the Gaussian 09 program [36]. The B3LYP [37,38] method with LANL2DZ basis set [39,40] is used for all atoms in the nanocomposites. B3LYP is a gradient-corrected hybrid DFT with exchange-correlation functional in Becke's three-parameter form, which includes a mixture of Hartree-Fock (HF) exchange with the Vosko, Wilk, and Nusair (VWN) functional III for local correlation and the correlation functional of Lee, Yang, and Parr (LYP) for non-local correlation. The default convergent criteria of Gaussian 09 are used for the energy and force constants. The optimized geometries of all nanocomposites are presented in Fig. 1(b). To confirm the energy stability of the structures, we also calculated the harmonic frequencies for all structures at the same theoretical level.

Based on the optimized geometries of nanocomposites, the optical absorption properties are calculated by employing the TD-DFT method. The optical spectra are obtained through the explicit solution of the time-dependent Kohn-Sham equations with a time-propagation [41,42]. This algorithm is particularly effective to treat the large nanocomposites [43,44] as the present ones. The TDDFT calculations are performed with Octopus software package [45]. The local-density approximation (LDA) [46] and Hartwigsen-Goedecker-Hutter (HGH) pseudopotential [47] are used to make the TD-DFT calculations feasible for nanocomposites. The time step is 0.002 hbar/eV and the maximum steps are 5000.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometrical Structures and Vibrational Spectra of QDs and Nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
The optimized geometrical structures of all the nanocomposites of the 10 macrocycles hybrid GQD are shown in Fig. 1(b). To evaluate the energy and chemical stabilities of the considered structures, the range of the harmonic frequencies are calculated and collected in Tables 1 and 2
. To visually demonstrate the vibrational spectra of the nanocomposites, we have plotted all the vibrational spectra in Fig. 2 . Both the tables and the figures demonstrate that the vibrational spectra of all the nanocomposites are positive, and no negative value occurs, which confirms that all the considered structures are stable in energy. The energy gap (E
g ) between HOMO and LUMO are calculated and presented in the two tables. The present E
g of GQD is 2.824 eV at the B3LYP/LANL2DZ theoretical level, which is larger than the 1.96 eV with standard DFT but smaller than the 4.68 eV with GW method [35]. The present E
g of GQD including 54 carbon atoms (plus 18 hydrogen atoms) is larger than the 1.10 eV of the hexagonal GQD atoms and 1.25 eV of the triangular GQD with 200 carbon atoms [16]. The E
g of the macrocycles from 2.077 eV to 2.408 eV, which is slightly larger than the theoretical with B3LYP/6-311G(d,p) in the literature [33]. On the whole, as the number of meso‑nitrogen atoms increases from zero to four, the E
g of the metal-free macrocycles decreases gradually, which means that the chemical stability slightly decreases. However, the Cu-metallated macrocycles have not shown the same trend.

From Table 2, one can find that the E
g s of the nanocomposites of GQD and the macrocycles are smaller as compared to that of the separate GQD itself. The values of E
g s of the nanocomposites are from 2.040 eV to 2.366 eV, which means that the nanocomposites are moderate chemical stability as the separate macrocycles. Meanwhile, the absorption edges of nanocomposites show obvious-shift, which is in good agreement with the experimental result of porphyrin-graphene nanohybrid of Xu et al. [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of Nanocomposites as Sensitizers of DSSC
ce:section.ce:para: The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the sensitizer can determine whether the nanocomposites are suitable for sensitizer of DSSC. As a sensitizer of DSSC, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels must satisfy the requirements of the electrode and electrolyte to transfer the electrons from the electrolyte to the LUMO of sensitizer and to the electrode. Taking the TiO2 electrode and the electrolyte of pair I−/I3 − as the example, we explore the feasibility of the present nanocomposites as the sensitizer. As the model of TiO2 electrode and solvent in the literature [49,50], the electronic properties of solid state TiO2 and continuum model of I−/I3 − electrolyte are used and the interaction between them and the nanocomposite are omitted in present examinations. The electron from the photoexcited sensitizer is injected into the conduction band minimum (CBM) of TiO2. The oxidized sensitizer is subsequently reduced by the electron donated by the electrolyte possessing of the pair I−/I3 −. Therefore, the successful electron transfer requires that the HOMO of the sensitizer must be very close to but lower than the redox energy level of I−/I3 − electrolyte (−4.80 eV), [51,52] while the LUMO must be higher than the energy level of CBM of TiO2 (−4.30 eV), to realize the electron injection from the LUMO to the electrode [53]. The energy levels of the HOMO and LUMO for all the considered nanocomposites, and the CBM of TiO2 and I−/I3 − redox energy level are demonstrated in Fig. 3 . The figure shows that the LUMO levels of all the considered nanocomposites are higher than the CBM of TiO2 and the HOMO levels are lower than the redox energy level of I−/I3 − electrolyte. For example, the LUMO levels of GQD-Pc (−3.2 eV) is higher than the −4.30 eV of the CBM of TiO2, which clearly indicates that the electrons can be injected into the electrode effectively. Furthermore, the HOMO energy level of GQD-Pc (−5.24 eV) is more negative than the −4.80 eV of redox energy level for I−/I3 − electrolyte. Therefore, all the energy levels of the present nanocomposites satisfy the requirement of DSSC.

Besides the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of sensitizer, there are other two factors significantly impacting the efficiency of DSSC. One is the electron transfer from macrocycle to the GQD, and the other is the spatial charge separation of carriers between the GQD and the macrocycle, which controls the electron-hole recombination rate. To understand the spatial charge separation of the GQD with macrocycles nanocomposites, we plot their HOMO and LUMO to demonstrate the contributor and acceptor of the absorption transition in Fig. 4 . Carefully checking the figure, one can find that the HOMO of GQD-Pc is localized on GQD, which implicates that the GQD is the contributor of the electron for the optical absorption of the two macrocycles. Meanwhile, the LUMO is localized on Pc as the acceptor. Therefore, there is obvious spatial charge separation in the nanocomposites of GQD-Pc. Interestingly, GQD-Cu-TBP demonstrates a similar spatial charge separation despite the different density distributions of HOMO and LUMO. As shown in Fig. 4, the HOMO of GQD-Cu-TBP is localized on Cu-TBP and the LUMO is localized on GQD, which gives different contributor and acceptor of electrons from those of GQD-Pc but the spatial charge separation is very obvious as that of GQD-Pc. As a result, the carrier recombination in the two nanocomposites is slow, which is very beneficial to the conversion efficiency of DSSC. Unfortunately, the obvious spatial charge separations are not true for the remaining nanocomposites, on which both HOMOs and LUMOs are localized on same parts of the nanocomposites. Therefore, the spatial charge separations in these nanocomposites are small. So, the recombination of charge carriers will be fast and the efficiency of DSSC is low. Therefore, we can conclude that GQD-Pc and GQD-Cu-TBP are the promising sensitizer for DSSC, but not the other nanocomposites considered at the present.

To understand the driving force for the electron transfer, we examined the rate of charge recombination processes that can be evaluated by the semiclassical model of Marcus electron-transfer theory [54].
(1)
k ab
=
V ab 2
π
λ
k B
T
ℏ 2
exp
−
λ + ∆ G
2
4 λ
k B
T
where k ab is the recombination rate constant, V ab is the electronic coupling between the initial and final states, which in turn depends on the overlap of HOMO and LUMO wave functions of the composite system, λ is the total reorganization energy, and ΔG is the Gibbs free energy change for the electron transfer process. The T, k B, and ℏ represent the temperature, Boltzmann constant, and the reduced Planck constant, respectively. For a specified temperature, the expression obviously demonstrates that the electron-hole recombination rate largely depends on V ab. Moreover, V ab is determined by the overlap HOMO and LUMO densities. For GQD-Pc and GQD-Cu-TBP nanocomposites, the electronic densities of HOMO and LUMO are obvious different, as shown in Fig. 4, which implies the overlaps between the electronic densities of the orbitals, which will result in small V ab and low electron-hole recombination rate. Therefore, we can conclude at least qualitatively, the rates of charge recombination for the two nanocomposites are quite low, which are suitable for sensitizers of DSSC. The energy offset can be used to predict the dynamics of electron transfer, which is also a very interesting feature for the electron transfer process [16]. As the E
g is the driving force for the electron transfer, the larger E
g could be expected because it can give larger driving force for the electron transfer. In Table 2, one can find that the E
g s of the nanocomposites are from 2.040 eV to 2.366 eV, which implies that the driving forces of the nanocomposites are almost same and suitable for the electron transfer.

The strong optical absorption in the visible light range is another factor to an efficient sensitizer of DSSC. To obtain the strength of the optical absorption of the nanocomposites, we perform TD-DFT calculations by using the optimized geometrical structures of nanocomposites. In the TD-DFT calculations, the B3LYP for the large nanocomposites is too much demand on computing resources to perform although it is available to the smaller nanocomposites. Therefore, LDA is used to all TD-DFT calculations to compare the results each other. The calculated integrated intensity (ƒ) of each structure is presented in Fig. 5(a) and (b).

As shown in Fig. 5(a), the first absorption peak of the separate GQD is near 2.5 eV, where is lower than that of Li et al. [35] with GW + BSE method because the LDA method underestimates the E
g in comparison of the GW. From an overall perspective, as the number of meso-nitrogen atoms increases from zero to four, it is observed that the absorption of metal-free macrocycles increased gradually. The first absorption peaks of metal-free macrocycles appear are close to those of Cammidge et al. by using UV/V absorption spectra in 2011 [55], while the present values of the first absorption peaks of the Cu-metallated macrocycles are close to the experimental ones of van As et al. in 2015 [33]. Therefore, the present calculational results of absorptions for the nanocomposites are acceptable and can be used to further analysis. The similar behavior can be seen from Fig. 5 for the two types of macrocycles. As the number of meso‑nitrogen atoms increases from zero to four, the absorptions of all macrocycles also increased gradually.

Now, let us throw light on the absorption characteristic of the nanocomposites. For all the nanocomposites, the absorption edges that are relative to the band gaps (E gs), show obvious red-shifts (from 2.0 eV to 1.5 eV approximately), as shown in Fig. 5(a) and (b). The obvious enhancement absorptions of the GQD-metal-free macrocycles nanocomposites are identified as Fig. 5(a). The nanocomposite of GQD-Pc should be a favorable sensitizer by the stronger absorption than that of the separate GQD or Pc in the visible light range. It should be noted that the remaining four nanocomposites of GQD-metal-free macrocycles are lack of spatial charge separation although their optical absorptions are stronger than that of GQD-Pc. Therefore, these nanocomposites are suitable for high-efficiency materials for harvesting solar energy but not suitable for sensitizers.

The optical absorption performances of GQD-Cu-metallated macrocycles nanocomposites show obviously different from those of the nanocomposites with the metal-free macrocycles. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the optical absorptions of all the nanocomposites are obviously enhanced in the visible light range. Fortunately, the nanocomposite of GQD-Cu-TBP with the strongest optical absorption in the visible light range is just the only one satisfy the requirement of HOMO and LUMO as a DSSC. Moreover, one can find that the absorption of GQD-Cu-TBP is also obviously stronger than that of the nanocomposite of GQD-Pc by comparing Fig. 5(a) and (b). In this sense, the GQD-Cu-TBP should be more efficient than GQD-Pc as a sensitizer.

In summary, the geometrical structures and electronic properties of the nanocomposites comprising of GQD and Pc, TBP, TBTAP, TBDAP, TBMAP and their Cu-metallated macrocycles are investigated at B3LYP/LANL2DZ theoretical level, while their optical absorption properties are calculated by using TD-DFT and HGH pseudo potentials. The stable structures of the nanocomposites are obtained by full relaxation and harmonic frequency analysis. The results indicate that all the nanocomposites are stable in energy and mediate chemical stability. All the nanocomposites demonstrate obvious absorption in the infrared range through the nuclear vibration. The TDDFT results demonstrate that optical absorptions of all the considered nanocomposites are significantly enhanced in the visible range, implying they are promising candidates for designing solar energy harvesting materials. The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of all nanocomposites satisfy the requirements of typical DSSC with the electrode of TiO2 and I−/I3 − electrolyte. However, only GQD-Pc and GQD-Cu-TBP demonstrate obvious spatial charge separation with which the recombination rates of their electron and hole are slow, implicating these two nanocomposites are favorable for sensitizer of DSSC. Moreover, the optical absorptions of GQD-Cu-TBP are stronger than that of GQD-Pc in the visible light range. Therefore, GQD-Cu-TBP, by virtue of the strongest absorption in the visible light range, obvious charge spatial separation, suitable LUMO energy levels, and suitable driving force, is the most promising candidate as a feasible sensitizer of DSSC. These findings are helpful for the design of the solar harvest materials with nanocomposites, especially for developing efficient sensitizer of DSSC.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under Grant No. 11574125 and No. NSFC-11374132, as well as the Taishan scholar project of Shandong province (ts201511055).
